description: 50-case diverse frozen decision corpus: 9 base (politics/geopolitics) + 21 corporate/regulatory/legal/labor + 20 civic/institutional/monetary/personal. Real events only, evidence frozen pre-decision, labels never enter prompts. Curator confidence flags preserved in the eval report.
leakage_rule: evidence[].date <= as_of < actual_action_date for every case; the harness asserts this and never renders actual_action into any actor input.

cases.situation: The Cuban missile crisis is at its most dangerous hour. The US naval quarantine holds, a U-2 was shot down over Cuba today, US invasion preparations are visible, and private letters have been exchanged with Kennedy in which he offers a non-invasion pledge if the missiles are withdrawn; your own second letter demanded Turkey missile withdrawal too, and back-channel word suggests Jupiter missiles in Turkey would quietly go later. Your generals urge standing firm; the risk of uncontrollable escalation is immediate.

commitments.statement: Publicly committed to defending Cuba against invasion

evidence.text: Kennedy announced a naval quarantine of Cuba and demanded removal of Soviet missiles.
Khrushchev's private first letter offered withdrawal in exchange for a US non-invasion pledge.
A U-2 reconnaissance plane was shot down over Cuba; the pilot was killed.
Robert Kennedy privately conveyed that Jupiter missiles in Turkey would be removed later, but never as a public trade, and that time was nearly out.
US invasion preparations in Florida were unmistakable; Castro cabled urging resolve.

cases.source_note: Withdrawal announced on Radio Moscow, 1962-10-28 (public record).

cases.situation: The 'smoking gun' tape released on August 5 shows you directing the cover-up six days after the break-in. Your remaining congressional defenders have collapsed; senior Republican senators told you today that fewer than fifteen senators would vote to acquit. Impeachment in the House is certain. Your family urges fighting on; your counsel and chief of staff see no path.
goals: protect the presidency as an institution

commitments.statement: Repeatedly declared publicly he would not resign

evidence.text: The Supreme Court ordered release of the White House tapes, 8-0.
The House Judiciary Committee approved three articles of impeachment.
The June 23, 1972 'smoking gun' transcript was released, showing obstruction from the start.
Nearly all remaining Republican support in Congress publicly evaporated.
Senators Goldwater and Scott and Rep. Rhodes told the president perhaps 15 Senate votes remained.

cases.source_note: Resignation announced in a national address, 1974-08-08, effective 08-09.

cases.situation: This morning a self-declared State Committee on the State of Emergency announced it has taken power, claiming Gorbachev is ill and isolated in Crimea. Tanks are moving into Moscow. You are the freshly and directly elected president of the Russian republic. The plotters have not arrested you yet; troops' loyalty is uncertain; crowds are beginning to gather at the White House.

commitments.statement: Elected on a democratic-sovereignty platform two months earlier

evidence.text: State television announced the emergency committee and claimed Gorbachev was incapacitated.
Armored columns entered central Moscow; key ministries were occupied.
The plotters had not moved to arrest the Russian republic's leadership at the White House.
Elements of the Taman division near the White House appeared hesitant; some crews fraternized with civilians.
Yeltsin won Russia's first direct presidential election with 57 percent.

cases.source_note: Climbed a tank outside the White House and called for general resistance and strikes, 1991-08-19 midday.

cases.situation: You won the first leadership ballot 204-152 but fell four votes short of the required margin, forcing a second ballot. You declared last night you would fight on. Today, one by one, your own cabinet ministers told you privately that while they support you, you cannot win and staying risks letting Heseltine take the party. Your husband and closest advisers counsel withdrawal.

commitments.statement: Publicly announced 'I fight on, I fight to win' after the first ballot

evidence.text: First ballot: 204 votes to Heseltine's 152 — four short of outright victory; a second ballot was triggered.
Thatcher, in Paris, declared she would contest the second ballot.
In sequential private meetings, most cabinet ministers advised she would lose the second ballot and should withdraw in favor of a stop-Heseltine candidate.
Geoffrey Howe resigned; his November 13 resignation speech devastated her position on Europe.

cases.source_note: Announced withdrawal from the second ballot to the cabinet on the morning of 1990-11-22.

cases.situation: You signed a $44 billion merger agreement for Twitter in April, then tried to terminate over bot counts. Twitter sued for specific performance in Delaware; trial begins October 17. Discovery has gone badly — your own texts are public, the judge has signaled skepticism of the bot defense, and your deposition is imminent. Financing remains committed at $54.20. Losing at trial would compel the same purchase with added litigation damage.
goals: control the asset if forced to buy

commitments.statement: Signed merger agreement at $54.20 per share with specific-performance clause

evidence.text: Musk filed to terminate the merger over spam accounts; Twitter sued for specific performance.
Chancellor McCormick largely sided with Twitter in discovery rulings; trial set for October 17.
Embarrassing private texts about the deal were released in court filings.
Musk's deposition was imminent and the bot-count defense was widely reported as weak.

cases.source_note: On 2022-10-04 Musk's lawyers proposed closing at the original $54.20 terms; the deal closed October 27.

cases.situation: Following the Pincher affair and your changing accounts of what you knew, Sunak and Javid resigned within minutes of each other yesterday evening, triggering a cascade — around fifty ministers and aides have now quit. A delegation of cabinet ministers waited in Downing Street tonight to tell you the position is unrecoverable. You dismissed Gove and vowed to fight on. The 1922 Committee is moving to change no-confidence rules.

commitments.statement: Told colleagues repeatedly he would not resign and had a fresh mandate of 14 million votes

evidence.text: Chancellor Sunak and Health Secretary Javid resigned over the Pincher affair and trust in the PM's account.
Dozens of ministerial resignations followed through the day; replacements were increasingly hard to find.
A cabinet delegation told the PM in person that he had lost the party and should go.
Johnson sacked Michael Gove and publicly insisted he would carry on.
He had survived a confidence vote 211-148, with rules barring another for a year — rules the 1922 Committee could change.

cases.source_note: Announced resignation as party leader outside No. 10 on 2022-07-07, staying as caretaker PM.

cases.situation: Russia's full-scale invasion began yesterday. Russian armored columns are converging on Kyiv; airborne assaults hit Hostomel airport; saboteur teams are reported hunting the government leadership. Western partners assess the capital may fall within days and the United States has offered to evacuate you to continue government from safety. Your security services say your life is the top target.

commitments.statement: Sworn to defend Ukraine's constitution and independence

evidence.text: Russia launched a full-scale invasion on multiple axes; airborne forces contested Hostomel near Kyiv.
Fighting reached Kyiv's outskirts; Western assessments gave the capital days at most.
The United States offered to evacuate the president; allied capitals prepared for a government-in-exile.
Reports circulated of assassination teams tasked against the leadership.

cases.source_note: Refused evacuation ('I need ammunition, not a ride' as widely reported) and filmed himself with ministers in central Kyiv on 2022-02-25 evening.

cases.situation: Tomorrow you open Parliament. Apartheid's costs are unsustainable: sanctions bite, the townships are ungovernable, the Berlin Wall's fall has removed the communist-threat rationale, and secret talks with the imprisoned Mandela and the exiled ANC have matured. Your security establishment warns against legalizing the ANC and SACP; business and much of your caucus quietly favor a decisive break. The world expects reform signals; the scale is your choice.
goals: a negotiated settlement from a position of relative strength

commitments.statement: Promised a 'new South Africa' on taking office in 1989

evidence.text: De Klerk met Mandela in prison; separate exploratory channels with the exiled ANC continued.
The Berlin Wall fell, collapsing the anti-communist rationale for repression.
Senior ANC prisoners including Sisulu were released without banning orders.
Speculation mounted that the February 2 address would announce significant reform; its scope was unknown.

cases.source_note: The 1990-02-02 address unbanned the ANC, PAC and SACP and announced Mandela's release.

cases.situation: Disney's November 8 earnings shocked the market: streaming losses of $1.5 billion, the stock at multi-year lows. CEO Chapek's credibility with creative leadership, investors and the press has collapsed; CFO McCarthy has privately signaled lost confidence to directors. Bob Iger, retired eleven months, is understood to be willing to return. Chapek's contract was renewed only in June; replacing him now is an admission of error, but the drift is compounding.

commitments.statement: Renewed Chapek's contract for three years in June 2022

evidence.text: Q4 earnings revealed $1.5B streaming losses; shares fell to multi-year lows.
A cost-cutting memo failed to reassure; senior creative executives' discontent with Chapek was widely reported.
CFO Christine McCarthy privately expressed lost confidence in Chapek to board members (as later reported).
The board had unanimously extended Chapek's contract through 2024.

cases.source_note: The board replaced Chapek with Iger on the evening of 2022-11-20.

cases.situation: Ten weeks ago the company replaced its 99-year-old flagship formula with a sweeter reformulation and retired the original. Instead of the expected win, a consumer revolt has built: organized protest groups, a flooded complaint hotline, angry bottlers, and relentless negative press. Executives face a choice between standing behind the reformulation they called a triumph or reversing course and bringing the old taste back.
goals: reverse the sales and public-relations crisis

commitments.statement: Publicly declared the reformulated flagship was permanent and the original would not return

evidence.text: Coca-Cola replaced its 99-year-old formula with a sweeter reformulation and discontinued the original.
Organized consumer protests grew, including a group publicly demanding the original formula's return.
The company's consumer hotline was flooded with tens of thousands of angry calls and letters; bottlers pressed for action.
Press coverage widely framed the reformulation as a marketing blunder; loyalists hoarded remaining old-formula stock.

cases.source_note: On 1985-07-11 Coca-Cola announced the return of the original formula as 'Coca-Cola Classic,' reversing course under consumer pressure.

cases.situation: The cloud-gaming service has struggled since launch: thin library, low adoption, and the earlier shutdown of its in-house studios. Rumors of closure have circulated for months, which the team publicly denied. Leadership must decide whether to keep funding the platform, pivot it, or shut it down and refund customers.
goals: cut losses on an underperforming service

commitments.statement: Publicly stated the service was not shutting down

evidence.text: Google launched Stadia to lukewarm reviews and a limited game library.
Google closed its in-house Stadia game studios, ending first-party development.
Stadia's official account publicly stated the service was 'not shutting down' amid rumors.
Developers and users reported dwindling new releases and low engagement on the platform.

cases.source_note: On 2022-09-29 Google announced it would shut down Stadia in January 2023 and refund hardware and software purchases.

cases.situation: After years of defending an independent third-party fact-checking program on its platforms, the company faces a changed political environment and persistent accusations of censorship from the right. The incoming U.S. administration has been openly hostile to platform moderation, and the CEO has been signaling accommodation. He must decide whether to keep the fact-checking system, adjust it, or scrap it in favor of a user-driven model.
goals: reduce moderation controversy and censorship criticism
improve ties with the incoming administration

commitments.statement: Long publicly defended the independent third-party fact-checking program as vital against misinformation

evidence.text: Donald Trump won the U.S. presidential election, reshaping the political landscape for large platforms.
Zuckerberg met Trump at Mar-a-Lago as the company signaled a more conciliatory posture.
The company donated $1 million to the incoming president's inaugural fund.
The company elevated a Republican policy veteran to lead global affairs as its prior policy chief departed.

cases.source_note: On 2025-01-07 the company announced it would end its third-party fact-checking program in the U.S. and replace it with a community-notes model.

cases.situation: The brand is mid-transition to an all-electric, upmarket relaunch and has wound down most of its existing model range. Leadership has been promising an uncompromising reinvention rather than incremental change. A brand communications rollout is imminent, and leadership must choose whether to debut a radical new identity now, hold the current identity, or delay.
goals: reposition the marque as an exclusive electric luxury brand

commitments.statement: Committed under its Reimagine strategy to relaunch the brand as an all-electric luxury marque

evidence.text: Under its Reimagine strategy, the parent group committed to relaunching the brand as an all-electric luxury marque.
The company reaffirmed that its combustion-era lineup would be retired as it moved to an electric-only range.
The brand wound down sales of most existing models in core markets ahead of the relaunch.
Executives publicly promised a bold, uncompromising reinvention of the brand rather than incremental change.

cases.source_note: On 2024-11-19 the brand unveiled a radical new visual identity and 'Copy Nothing' campaign ahead of its December concept-car reveal.

cases.situation: Two years ago the company dropped 'HBO' from its flagship streaming service and renamed it, arguing the shorter name signaled a broader content range; executives defended the move publicly. Since then the service has leaned heavily on prestige originals in its marketing. With an annual investor and advertiser presentation imminent, leadership must decide whether to keep the current name, restore the old premium branding, or make some other change.
goals: reassociate the service with a premium reputation

commitments.statement: Publicly defended dropping 'HBO' from the streaming brand as a deliberate strategy

evidence.text: The company announced it would drop 'HBO' from the flagship streaming brand and rename the service.
The service relaunched under the shorter name, combining prestige and unscripted libraries.
Executives publicly defended removing 'HBO,' saying the shorter name signaled a broader content range.
The company leaned on prestige originals in marketing as subscriber growth tracked those titles.

cases.source_note: On 2025-05-14 the company announced at its upfront that it would restore the 'HBO Max' name, reversing the 2023 rebrand.

cases.situation: The company signed a roughly $20 billion agreement to buy a design-software rival more than a year ago and has repeatedly voiced confidence it would close. UK and EU regulators have since turned sharply negative, and the company has resisted offering divestitures, arguing there is no workable remedy. With clearance looking unlikely and a large break fee on the line, leadership must decide whether to keep fighting for the deal or abandon it.
goals: avoid a costly drawn-out regulatory fight
protect the core business and reputation

commitments.statement: Signed a definitive merger agreement to acquire the target for about $20 billion

evidence.text: The company agreed to acquire a design-software rival for about $20 billion.
EU regulators issued a formal statement of objections raising serious competition concerns.
The UK competition regulator provisionally found the deal would harm competition in design software.
The company reportedly declined to offer divestitures, arguing no viable remedy existed.

cases.source_note: On 2023-12-18 the two companies mutually terminated the acquisition, with the acquirer paying a $1 billion break fee, citing regulatory obstacles.

cases.situation: Weeks after signing a memorandum to explore a merger with a larger domestic rival, the talks have soured over structure. The larger partner reportedly wants to fold the company in as a subsidiary rather than merge as equals, which the board resists. Facing deep financial trouble of its own, leadership must decide whether to accept the revised structure, push back, or walk away from the talks entirely.
goals: secure scale to survive a brutal EV transition
protect the company's autonomy and identity

commitments.statement: Signed a non-binding memorandum of understanding to explore a merger

evidence.text: The company announced deep cost cuts including thousands of job losses and capacity reductions amid falling profit.
The two automakers signed a memorandum to explore a merger via a joint holding company.
Reports said the larger partner proposed making the company a subsidiary, which the board resisted.
Reports indicated the two sides remained far apart over the deal's structure.

cases.source_note: On 2025-02-13 the companies announced they had terminated the merger talks after the structure dispute could not be resolved.

cases.situation: Last year the company rebuffed a roughly $23 billion buyout approach from a major cloud provider, with its chief executive telling staff it would stay independent and pursue an IPO. Now the same suitor is back with a far larger offer and the two are reportedly in advanced talks. Leadership must decide whether to hold to the independent path it publicly championed or accept an acquisition.
goals: maximize value for founders and investors
sustain rapid growth with a strong parent or independently

commitments.statement: Publicly indicated it intended to remain independent and pursue an IPO

evidence.text: The company raised about $1 billion at roughly a $12 billion valuation and set aggressive revenue targets.
Reports said the company walked away from an acquisition offer of about $23 billion from a major cloud provider.
The chief executive told staff the company would stay independent and head toward an IPO.
Renewed reports said the suitor and the company were again in advanced acquisition talks.

cases.source_note: On 2025-03-18 the company agreed to be acquired by the cloud provider for about $32 billion, its largest deal to date.

cases.situation: Four days ago the board abruptly removed the chief executive and, after reinstatement talks stalled, installed an interim leader. The backlash has been overwhelming: a major backer offered to hire the ousted executive and nearly all staff have signed a letter threatening to quit unless the board reverses course and resigns. The directors must decide whether to hold firm on the removal or agree to bring the executive back under a reconstituted board.
goals: preserve the organization's mission and stability

commitments.statement: Publicly removed the chief executive and installed interim leadership

evidence.text: The board abruptly removed the chief executive, saying it had lost confidence in him; the president then quit.
Talks to bring the ousted executive back stalled and the board appointed an interim chief executive.
A major backer said it would hire the ousted executive and colleagues to lead a new group.
Around 700 of roughly 770 employees signed a letter threatening to resign unless the board reinstated the executive and stepped down.

cases.source_note: On 2023-11-22 the organization announced the chief executive would return under a new board, and the directors who removed him stepped down.

cases.situation: The chip maker's turnaround under its current chief executive has stalled: deep losses, a suspended dividend, mass layoffs, a collapsing share price, and removal from a marquee stock index. The board has been reviewing strategic options and is reportedly frustrated with the slow pace of recovery. Directors must decide whether to stay the course with the current leader or force a change at the top.
goals: revive the manufacturing and foundry strategy

evidence.text: The company reported a large quarterly loss, suspended its dividend, and announced roughly 15% workforce cuts.
The board reviewed strategic options amid a collapsing share price and doubts about the turnaround.
The company was removed from a marquee stock index and replaced by a chip rival.
Reports described board frustration with the slow pace of the turnaround.

cases.source_note: On 2024-12-02 the company announced the chief executive had departed and named two interim co-leaders, following board pressure over the turnaround.

cases.situation: The coffee chain's sales have slipped and its outlook has been cut, and activist investors have built stakes and are pressing for change. The chief executive has been in the role only about a year. The board must decide whether to back its leader through the turnaround, wait, or move quickly to replace him.

evidence.text: The company reported a surprise decline in sales and cut its outlook; shares fell.
An activist investor built a sizable stake and pushed for changes.
Pressure mounted over the summer as a second activist reportedly took a stake.
The chief executive, about a year into the role, faced growing doubts about the turnaround.

cases.source_note: On 2024-08-13 the company replaced its chief executive with the head of a rival restaurant chain, effective the following month.

cases.situation: A newly announced per-install runtime fee for the company's game engine has ignited a fierce developer revolt: threats to abandon the engine, public condemnation, and even office closures after threats. Leadership initially defended the pricing but has now apologized and promised changes. It must decide whether to hold the policy, scrap it, or substantially revise it.
goals: retain developer trust and the ecosystem

commitments.statement: Publicly defended the new runtime-fee pricing as fair when first announced

evidence.text: The company announced a new per-install runtime fee starting in 2024, triggering developer backlash.
Widespread developer condemnation followed; some studios threatened to remove games or boycott the engine.
Offices briefly closed after threats amid the uproar.
The company publicly apologized and said it would make changes to the policy.

cases.source_note: On 2023-09-22 the company published a revised policy that capped the runtime fee, exempted existing games, and let developers self-report installs.

cases.situation: For decades the airline has marketed free checked bags as central to its brand and repeatedly defended the policy, even as an activist investor won board seats and pushed for new revenue. Under mounting margin pressure, leadership is weighing changes it long ruled out. It must decide whether to keep bags free, introduce fees, or make some lesser change.

commitments.statement: Repeatedly and publicly promised that checked bags would fly free

evidence.text: For years the airline marketed 'bags fly free' as a core brand differentiator.
At an investor day the airline defended its free-checked-bags policy while announcing other changes.
An activist investor secured multiple board seats after building a large stake, intensifying pressure for new revenue.
The airline reported continued margin pressure and signaled a search for new revenue sources.

cases.source_note: On 2025-03-11 the airline announced it would begin charging most passengers for checked bags, ending its signature free-bags policy.

cases.situation: Revenue is shrinking for the first time, the metaverse unit is losing billions, and the share price has collapsed, drawing open investor demands to cut costs and headcount. The company has already frozen hiring and warned of tighter budgets. Leadership must decide whether to keep the workforce intact, trim at the margins, or carry out large layoffs the company has never done before.

evidence.text: The company slowed hiring and told teams to plan for tighter budgets.
A prominent investor published an open letter urging the company to cut staff and rein in metaverse spending.
Quarterly results showed shrinking revenue and soaring losses in the metaverse unit; the stock fell sharply.
Reports described intensifying cost pressure and investor demands for spending discipline.

cases.source_note: On 2022-11-09 the company announced its first mass layoffs, cutting about 11,000 jobs, roughly 13% of staff.

cases.situation: The payments company expanded headcount rapidly during the pandemic e-commerce boom, then saw the environment turn: its internal valuation was cut, funding dried up, and the IPO window closed. Peers across tech are freezing hiring and cutting costs. Leadership must decide whether to hold headcount, trim modestly, or make a substantial workforce reduction.
goals: align costs with a tougher environment

evidence.text: The company cut its internal valuation sharply amid the fintech downturn.
Tech hiring freezes and cost cuts spread across the sector.
Post-pandemic normalization pressured payment volumes and growth.
The IPO market stayed frozen and late-stage startups faced pressure to reach profitability.

cases.source_note: On 2022-11-03 the company cut about 14% of its workforce, roughly 1,100 jobs, citing over-hiring and a tougher economy.

cases.situation: The regulator is nearing a final decision on a very large gaming acquisition. Its provisional findings warned of competition harm, though it later dropped its console theory while keeping concerns about the nascent cloud-gaming market. The acquirer has offered licensing deals to rivals as remedies. With a statutory deadline near, the regulator must clear the deal, clear it with remedies, or block it.

evidence.text: The regulator's provisional findings warned the deal could harm competition in consoles and cloud gaming.
The acquirer signed multi-year deals to bring a flagship title to rival platforms to address concerns.
The regulator narrowed its case, dropping the console theory but keeping cloud-gaming concerns.
The statutory deadline for a final decision approached in late April.

cases.source_note: On 2023-04-26 the regulator blocked the acquisition over concerns about competition in cloud gaming.

cases.situation: A foreign steelmaker's roughly $14.9 billion bid for a storied domestic producer has drawn heavy political opposition, including from the president, the main steel union, and both major presidential candidates. The national-security review panel has referred the matter without a consensus recommendation, leaving the decision to the president. He must approve the deal, seek more review, or block it.
goals: protect a domestically owned steel industry

evidence.text: A foreign steelmaker agreed to acquire a storied domestic producer for about $14.9 billion, prompting political opposition.
The president publicly stated the domestic producer should remain domestically owned and operated.
The main steel union and both major presidential candidates voiced opposition to the deal.
The national-security review panel referred the deal to the president without a consensus recommendation.

cases.source_note: On 2025-01-03 the president formally blocked the acquisition, citing national-security and supply-chain concerns.

cases.situation: A defamation suit over election-fraud broadcasts is about to go to trial. Pretrial rulings have gone badly: the judge found the underlying claims false as a matter of law, private messages showing internal doubt are public, and the court has sanctioned the company over discovery. Having publicly vowed to fight, the company must decide whether to go to trial or settle.
goals: protect executives and hosts from testimony

commitments.statement: Publicly vowed to fight the lawsuit and defended its coverage on First Amendment grounds

evidence.text: Court filings revealed private messages showing hosts and executives privately doubted the fraud claims.
The judge ruled the vote-rigging claims about the plaintiff were false as a matter of law, gutting the truth defense.
The judge sanctioned the company over withheld evidence and appointed a special master.
Jury selection began ahead of opening statements.

cases.source_note: On 2023-04-18 the company agreed to settle the defamation case for $787.5 million just as the trial was to begin.

cases.situation: Days after a securities regulator sued him for fraud over a tweet about taking the company private, the executive faces a threat to bar him from leading any public company. He had reportedly rejected a settlement, prompting the suit, and the stock has since dropped sharply. Advisers are urging him to settle. He must decide whether to fight the case or accept a deal.
goals: remain in control of the company
limit damage to the company and shareholders

commitments.statement: Publicly maintained the 'funding secured' claim was truthful and initially refused to settle

evidence.text: The executive tweeted he was considering taking the company private at $420 with 'funding secured.'
The securities regulator sued the executive for fraud, seeking to bar him from serving as an officer or director.
The company's shares fell about 14%; reports said the executive had rejected a settlement two days earlier.
Advisers and directors reportedly urged the executive to settle to remove the officer-bar threat.

cases.source_note: On 2018-09-29 the executive agreed to a revised settlement, stepping down as chairman and paying a fine, days after rejecting one.

cases.situation: Contracts with all three Detroit automakers expire at midnight on September 14 with the sides still far apart. Members have authorized a strike by a wide margin, and leadership has publicly demanded large raises and restored benefits and threatened to strike any company without a fair deal. The union must decide whether to keep talking past the deadline, extend, or strike.
goals: win large wage and benefit gains
honor the pledge made to members

commitments.statement: Publicly vowed to strike any automaker without a fair deal by the contract deadline

evidence.text: Union members voted overwhelmingly to authorize a strike if no deal was reached.
The union publicly demanded large raises and restored benefits and threatened to strike all three automakers.
Talks remained far apart with the contract deadline days away.
The union said it had not received a serious offer and was preparing for a possible strike.

cases.source_note: On 2023-09-15 the union launched simultaneous targeted strikes at plants of all three Detroit automakers.

cases.situation: Negotiators reached a tentative contract with the aircraft maker that leaders recommended, but many of the roughly 33,000 members are publicly rejecting it as inadequate after sixteen years without a new deal. A ratification-and-strike vote is imminent. The membership must decide whether to accept the tentative deal or reject it and walk out.
goals: win larger raises and restored pension
make up for years without a new contract

evidence.text: Company and union negotiators reached a tentative deal with about 25% raises; union leaders recommended acceptance.
Many rank-and-file members publicly called the offer inadequate, citing a lost pension and demands for larger raises.
Workers had gone about sixteen years without a new contract and many signaled they wanted to strike.
The financially strained aircraft maker warned that a strike would be damaging.

cases.source_note: On 2024-09-13 union members overwhelmingly rejected the tentative deal and walked out, beginning a strike.

cases.situation: You are Harvard's president. Since your December 5 congressional testimony on campus antisemitism, criticism has not subsided, and a widening set of plagiarism allegations against your academic work has drawn national attention. The Harvard Corporation publicly affirmed its confidence in you in mid-December, but donors, alumni, and some faculty remain in open revolt, and new complaints keep surfacing as the spring term approaches.
goals: avoid a precedent of donor-driven ousters

commitments.statement: The Harvard Corporation had publicly affirmed its confidence in Gay in mid-December

evidence.text: Gay testified before a House committee on antisemitism; her answer about context drew bipartisan condemnation.
The Harvard Corporation announced its unanimous support for Gay to remain president.
Harvard said Gay would request corrections to two papers amid mounting plagiarism allegations.
Additional plagiarism allegations were reported, intensifying public calls for her to step down.

cases.source_note: Gay resigned the Harvard presidency on 2024-01-02, the shortest tenure in the university's history.

cases.situation: You are the president of the University of Virginia. The federal Justice Department has pressed the university over its diversity programs and, according to reports, has signaled that your departure would help resolve a civil-rights inquiry that threatens federal funding. Your governing board, reshaped by gubernatorial appointees, has already dismantled diversity offices this year. Faculty and Virginia's US senators are urging you to stay; federal officials are pressing the other way.

evidence.text: UVA's board voted to dissolve the university's central diversity, equity, and inclusion office under state pressure.
Reports emerged that the Justice Department was pressuring UVA over diversity programs and Ryan's leadership.
The Justice Department reportedly signaled that Ryan's departure was sought to settle the civil-rights inquiry.
Senators Warner and Kaine publicly warned against forcing Ryan out of the presidency.

cases.source_note: Ryan announced his resignation as UVA president on 2025-06-27 amid federal pressure over diversity programs.

cases.situation: You lead Harvard University. The federal administration, having announced a review of billions of dollars in funding, has now sent a sweeping list of demands touching governance, admissions, hiring, and oversight of 'viewpoint diversity.' A peer institution recently agreed to several federal demands. Complying could preserve funding but cede institutional control; resisting risks an immediate freeze of research money.

evidence.text: Columbia agreed to a set of federal demands to restore funding, setting a precedent for peer schools.
The administration announced a review of about $9B in federal support to Harvard.
The administration sent Harvard an expanded list of demands on governance, admissions, and audits of 'viewpoint diversity.'
Faculty groups and the AAUP filed suit and urged the university to resist the demands.

cases.source_note: Harvard rejected the administration's demands on 2025-04-14 and sued after the government froze about $2.2B in funding.

cases.situation: You have led the National Rifle Association as its chief executive for three decades. A New York civil trial over alleged financial misconduct at the organization is about to begin, with you named as a central defendant. You have weathered internal revolts and legal threats before and have continued to run the NRA. Your health and the coming trial now weigh on the question of your role.

evidence.text: LaPierre survived an internal attempt to oust him at the NRA's annual meeting.
New York's attorney general sued to dissolve the NRA, alleging LaPierre diverted millions.
A New York court set the civil trial over NRA financial misconduct to begin January 8, 2024.
LaPierre remained the NRA's executive vice president as the trial date neared.

cases.source_note: LaPierre announced his resignation as NRA chief on 2024-01-05, effective January 31, days before his civil trial opened.

cases.situation: You lead the Susan G. Komen breast-cancer foundation. Two days ago your organization confirmed it was ending grants to Planned Parenthood, and the reaction has been ferocious: a torrent of criticism, a surge of donations to Planned Parenthood, a letter from more than two dozen senators, and public dissent from several of your own local affiliates. The foundation's leadership is under intense pressure over the decision.
goals: protect the foundation's mission and brand
stop the donor and volunteer exodus

commitments.statement: Komen had publicly defended the funding decision as apolitical

evidence.text: Komen confirmed it was cutting grants to Planned Parenthood for breast-cancer screenings.
A public backlash erupted and Planned Parenthood reported a large surge of replacement donations.
More than two dozen US senators sent a letter urging Komen to reverse the decision.
Several Komen affiliates publicly broke with the national decision.

cases.source_note: Komen reversed course on 2012-02-03, restoring Planned Parenthood's eligibility for grants after three days of backlash.

cases.situation: You are the Walt Disney Company. Five days ago your ABC network pulled a late-night program off the air indefinitely after the host's monologue about a political killing, amid pressure from a federal regulator and threats by major affiliate groups to drop the show. The suspension has triggered a fierce backlash of open letters, boycott threats, and subscriber cancellations, even as some station owners still refuse to carry the program.
goals: protect relationships with regulators and affiliates
avoid appearing to either cave or to censor

commitments.statement: Disney had pulled the show indefinitely, citing the host's monologue

evidence.text: ABC pulled 'Jimmy Kimmel Live!' indefinitely after the host's remarks about a high-profile political killing.
A federal regulator suggested consequences and two large affiliate groups said they would preempt the show.
Hundreds of performers and writers signed an open letter defending the host as boycott threats spread.
Free-speech groups, unions, and some shareholders pressed the company to reverse course.

cases.source_note: Disney returned 'Jimmy Kimmel Live!' to the air on 2025-09-23, reversing the indefinite suspension.

cases.situation: You run CBS and its parent, which is awaiting federal approval of a merger. Your flagship late-night program leads its time slot but late-night television is bleeding advertising revenue, and the show is reportedly unprofitable. Its host has just publicly attacked the company's recent legal settlement with the president. You must decide the program's future.

evidence.text: The parent company's merger with a studio awaited federal regulatory approval.
The company settled the president's lawsuit against '60 Minutes' for $16 million.
The late-night host called the settlement 'a big fat bribe' on air.
Late-night television faced steep advertising declines, and the program was reported to be unprofitable.

cases.source_note: CBS announced on 2025-07-17 that it would end 'The Late Show' in May 2026, calling it a financial decision.

cases.situation: You run the newly merged Warner Bros. Discovery, under a mandate to cut roughly three billion dollars in costs and to pivot away from streaming-only films toward theatrical releases. 'Batgirl,' a completed film made for about ninety million dollars for a streaming service, is in post-production, and test screenings reportedly disappointed. Earnings are days away, and you must decide what to do with the finished picture.
goals: reposition the film strategy toward theatrical

evidence.text: The Warner Bros.-Discovery merger closed; the new CEO pledged deep cost cuts.
'Batgirl,' an ~$90M film made for streaming, was in post-production.
Reported test screenings of 'Batgirl' underwhelmed executives.
Targeting ~$3B in savings, the studio was shifting from streaming-only films toward theatrical ahead of Aug 4 earnings.

cases.source_note: Warner Bros. Discovery shelved the finished 'Batgirl' film on 2022-08-02, opting never to release it.

cases.situation: You run basketball operations for the Dallas Mavericks. Your 25-year-old superstar led the team to the NBA Finals last spring and is eligible this summer for a five-year supermax extension the franchise is widely expected to offer. He has been sidelined by a calf injury, and there has been quiet friction over conditioning. You must decide how to handle his future with the team.
goals: manage long-term salary and roster risk

commitments.statement: The star was eligible for a supermax extension the Mavericks were widely expected to offer

evidence.text: Dončić led the Mavericks to the NBA Finals as the team's franchise centerpiece.
Dončić suffered a calf strain and was sidelined for several weeks.
Dončić was eligible that summer for a five-year supermax extension projected near $345 million.
The general manager had publicly described Dončić as the face of the franchise, and an extension was widely expected.

cases.source_note: Dallas traded Dončić to the Lakers in a shock deal reported on 2025-02-02.

cases.situation: You are Penn State's athletic leadership. Your football team began the season ranked in the top three but has just lost its third straight game, falling to .500 after upsets by an unranked opponent and, today, by Northwestern. Your head coach is signed to a long-term contract with a buyout reported near fifty million dollars. You must decide his status.

commitments.statement: The coach was signed to a long-term contract with a buyout reported near $50 million

evidence.text: Third-ranked Penn State lost in double overtime to Oregon, its first loss of the season.
Penn State was upset by a then-winless UCLA team.
Penn State lost to Northwestern, its third straight defeat, falling to 3-3.
The head coach's contract buyout was reported at roughly $50 million.

cases.source_note: Penn State fired head coach James Franklin on 2025-10-12 after a third straight loss.

cases.situation: You are the commissioner of the PGA Tour. For a year you have waged open war against a Saudi-backed rival golf circuit, suspending players who defected, fighting dueling antitrust lawsuits, and framing the conflict in stark moral terms. The litigation is entrenched and costly, and the Saudi sovereign wealth fund has effectively unlimited resources. You must decide the Tour's next move in the conflict.
goals: preserve the Tour's control of elite golf
end a costly legal and financial war

commitments.statement: The commissioner had publicly vowed the Tour would never do business with the Saudi fund or the rival circuit

evidence.text: The Saudi-backed LIV Golf circuit launched; the PGA Tour suspended participating players.
LIV players and the PGA Tour became locked in dueling antitrust litigation.
The commissioner had repeatedly cast the rivalry in moral terms, invoking families of 9/11 victims.
The legal fight appeared entrenched with no resolution in sight.

cases.source_note: The PGA Tour announced a shock framework agreement to partner with Saudi Arabia's PIF and LIV Golf on 2023-06-06.

cases.situation: You lead Israel's government. After fifteen months of war in Gaza, mediators announced yesterday that Israel and Hamas have reached a phased ceasefire-and-hostage-release agreement. Bringing hostages home is a national priority, but hard-right coalition partners threaten to bring down the government if the deal passes, and the leadership has long vowed to continue until 'total victory.' The cabinet must ratify or block the agreement.

commitments.statement: The prime minister had repeatedly vowed to fight until 'total victory' over Hamas

evidence.text: Earlier US-, Qatar-, and Egypt-brokered ceasefire rounds had repeatedly collapsed over terms.
Mediators announced Israel and Hamas had reached a phased ceasefire-and-hostage-release agreement.
A far-right minister threatened to quit the coalition if the deal was approved.
The prime minister said a last-minute dispute with Hamas had delayed the cabinet's ratification vote.

cases.source_note: Israel's cabinet approved the Gaza ceasefire-and-hostage-release deal on 2025-01-17.

cases.situation: You are the United States at the UN Security Council. Algeria has advanced a resolution recommending full UN membership for the State of Palestine, and a vote is scheduled this week. Your longstanding position is that Palestinian statehood should be achieved through direct negotiations rather than a Security Council vote, but most other members support the measure and blocking it will draw regional and diplomatic criticism.

commitments.statement: The US had long held that Palestinian statehood must come through direct negotiations

evidence.text: Algeria circulated a draft resolution recommending full UN membership for the State of Palestine.
The Security Council's admissions committee failed to reach a consensus recommendation.
US officials reiterated that Palestinian statehood should come through direct negotiations.
A Security Council vote on the membership resolution was scheduled for later that week.

cases.source_note: The United States vetoed the Palestinian UN membership resolution on 2024-04-18.

cases.situation: You are the US administration. A week ago you imposed sweeping 'reciprocal' tariffs on nearly every trading partner; markets have plunged, wiping trillions from global equities, and higher country-specific rates are set to take effect within days. Business leaders, foreign governments, and some allies are pressing for relief, while you have publicly insisted the tariffs will stay. You must decide the next step.
goals: reset trade terms in its favor
avoid a market and economic crisis

commitments.statement: The president had publicly insisted the tariffs would remain and would not be paused

evidence.text: The administration announced sweeping 'reciprocal' tariffs on nearly all trading partners.
Global markets plunged, wiping trillions from equities over two days.
A baseline 10% tariff took effect, with higher country-specific rates set for April 9.
The president publicly rejected calls to pause the tariffs and urged countries to negotiate.

cases.source_note: The administration announced a 90-day pause on most reciprocal tariffs on 2025-04-09 while raising tariffs on China.

cases.situation: You are the Bank of Japan's policy board. Inflation has run at or above your two-percent target for many months, and the spring wage negotiations have just produced the largest pay increases in over three decades, the confirmation you have said you needed. Ending the world's last negative-interest-rate policy and yield-curve control would be historic; moving too soon risks choking a fragile recovery. You meet this week.
goals: normalize policy without derailing the recovery
maintain credibility on the inflation target

commitments.statement: The BOJ had guided that normalization required confirmation of sustained wage growth

evidence.text: Japan's inflation had held at or above the 2% target for many months.
Japan's largest labor federation reported the biggest annual wage increases in over three decades.
Multiple reports said the BOJ board was leaning toward ending negative rates at the March meeting.
Markets priced a strong chance the BOJ would exit negative rates and scrap yield-curve control.

cases.source_note: The Bank of Japan ended negative interest rates and yield-curve control on 2024-03-19, its first rate hike since 2007.

cases.situation: You are Greece's prime minister. Days ago you called a referendum in which voters resoundingly rejected the creditors' bailout terms, and your finance minister then resigned. Yet the banks are shuttered, the economy is seizing, and the creditors are offering a new loan only on terms as harsh as those just rejected, with euro membership itself now in question. A eurozone summit will decide Greece's fate.
goals: reopen the banks and stabilize the economy

commitments.statement: Tsipras had campaigned for the 'No' vote against the creditors' terms

evidence.text: Greek voters rejected the creditors' bailout terms in a referendum, backing Tsipras's 'No' call.
Finance Minister Varoufakis resigned the morning after the vote.
Greece formally requested a new three-year ESM loan.
Athens submitted reform proposals close to the creditors' demands to remain in the euro.

cases.source_note: Tsipras accepted a new three-year bailout on harsh terms at the 2015-07-13 eurozone summit despite the 'No' vote.

cases.situation: You are UBS. Over the weekend Swiss authorities have pressed you to take over your stricken rival Credit Suisse, whose shares collapsed after its largest shareholder ruled out more capital and whose central-bank liquidity backstop failed to stop client outflows. A deal before markets open Monday could avert a wider crisis, but the target carries enormous risks and legal exposures. You must decide before the open.
goals: protect its own balance sheet and shareholders

evidence.text: Credit Suisse shares plunged after its largest shareholder ruled out providing more capital.
The Swiss National Bank extended a CHF 50B liquidity backstop that failed to halt outflows.
Swiss authorities pressed for a UBS takeover of Credit Suisse before markets reopened.
Deposit and asset outflows from Credit Suisse accelerated as a weekend rescue was negotiated.

cases.source_note: UBS agreed to acquire Credit Suisse in a state-brokered rescue announced 2023-03-19.

cases.situation: You are the President of the United States and your party's presumptive nominee. Since your widely panned debate performance in late June, a swelling chorus of your own party's lawmakers and donors has urged you to leave the race, and reports say senior leaders have privately told you your path is collapsing. You have repeatedly insisted you will stay in, and you are now isolating after a COVID-19 diagnosis. You must decide whether to continue your candidacy.
goals: give his party the best chance to win

commitments.statement: Biden had repeatedly and publicly insisted he would remain in the race

evidence.text: Biden's halting debate performance against Trump triggered widespread alarm in his party.
More than a dozen additional congressional Democrats had publicly called for him to step aside.
Reports said senior Democrats, including Pelosi and Schumer, had privately warned Biden his path was collapsing.
Biden tested positive for COVID-19 and went into isolation at his Delaware home.

cases.source_note: Biden withdrew from the presidential race on 2024-07-21 and endorsed Kamala Harris.

cases.situation: You are a former White House strategist. The House committee investigating January 6 has subpoenaed you for documents and testimony, with deadlines this week. The former president's lawyer has urged ex-aides to refuse cooperation on executive-privilege grounds, and the committee has signaled it will pursue criminal contempt against anyone who defies its demands. You must decide how to respond to the subpoena.
goals: align with the former president's stance

commitments.statement: Bannon had signaled through counsel he would resist the subpoena on privilege grounds

evidence.text: The House January 6 committee subpoenaed Bannon for documents and testimony.
The former president's lawyer urged ex-aides to refuse cooperation, citing executive privilege.
Bannon's attorney said he would not comply until privilege issues were resolved or a court ruled.
The committee signaled it would pursue criminal contempt for non-compliance.

cases.source_note: Bannon defied the January 6 committee subpoena by the 2021-10-14 deadline and was later convicted of contempt of Congress.

cases.situation: You are the governor of Virginia. Yesterday a photograph from your 1984 medical-school yearbook page, showing one person in blackface and another in a Ku Klux Klan hood, was published, and you issued a statement apologizing for appearing in it. Overnight, calls for your resignation have poured in from across your own party, including presidential candidates. You face the state's press today and must decide your course.

commitments.statement: Northam had issued a statement the previous day apologizing for appearing in the photo

evidence.text: A photo from Northam's 1984 medical-school yearbook page, showing blackface and a KKK hood, was published.
Northam issued a statement apologizing and acknowledging he was in the photo.
Numerous Democratic officials and 2020 presidential candidates called on him to resign.
Virginia's political establishment widely expected his resignation within days.

cases.source_note: Northam reversed his apology and refused to resign at a 2019-02-02 press conference, denying he was in the photo.
